texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE (CKD) NUTRITIONAL RECOMMENDATIONS FOR PERITONEAL DIALYSIS
texts.text: CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE (CKD) NUTRITIONAL RECOMMENDATIONS FOR PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: E-Library Reference

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline clinical content
texts.text: Guideline clinical content

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Clinical Guidelines assist in decision-making; they do not replace clinical judgement. Regardless of the strength  of  evidence,  it  remains  the  responsibility  of  the  clinician  to  interpret  the  application  of  the clinical  guidance to local circumstances and the needs and wishes of the individual patient.  Where variations of any kind do occur, it is important to document the variations and the reason for them in the patient's health record. If in doubt, seek senior advice.
texts.text: Clinical Guidelines assist in decision-making; they do not replace clinical judgement. Regardless of the strength  of  evidence,  it  remains  the  responsibility  of  the  clinician  to  interpret  the  application  of  the clinical  guidance to local circumstances and the needs and wishes of the individual patient.  Where variations of any kind do occur, it is important to document the variations and the reason for them in the patient's health record. If in doubt, seek senior advice.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Introduction
texts.text: Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This document is for renal dietitians and other healthcare professionals who care for people with stage 5 CKD who need renal replacement therapy, specifically peritoneal dialysis (PD).
texts.text: This document is for renal dietitians and other healthcare professionals who care for people with stage 5 CKD who need renal replacement therapy, specifically peritoneal dialysis (PD).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  All patients should be assessed by a renal dietitian when they commence PD.
texts.text:  All patients should be assessed by a renal dietitian when they commence PD.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Thereafter patients should be reviewed either indirectly or directly every 6 months. A direct (face to face) review should be carried out annually. More frequent review may be required dependant on blood results and identified nutritional needs.
texts.text:  Thereafter patients should be reviewed either indirectly or directly every 6 months. A direct (face to face) review should be carried out annually. More frequent review may be required dependant on blood results and identified nutritional needs.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  All patients should be screened for undernutrition using the nutrition screening guidelines.
texts.text:  All patients should be screened for undernutrition using the nutrition screening guidelines.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  The dietitian will participate in the multi-disciplinary PD Quality Assurance (PDQA) clinics (3 per month), during which Subjective Global Assessment (SGA) and Handgrip Strength (HGS) measurements will be carried out where possible, and patients requiring further review will be identified.
texts.text:  The dietitian will participate in the multi-disciplinary PD Quality Assurance (PDQA) clinics (3 per month), during which Subjective Global Assessment (SGA) and Handgrip Strength (HGS) measurements will be carried out where possible, and patients requiring further review will be identified.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Summary
texts.text: Summary

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Recommendations for guideline content
texts.text: Recommendations for guideline content

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ideal Body Weight (IBW) should be calculated to determine nutritional requirements. Appendix C (i) gives guidance on this.
texts.text: Ideal Body Weight (IBW) should be calculated to determine nutritional requirements. Appendix C (i) gives guidance on this.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Energy: 30 - 35 Kcal/Kg IBW/day
texts.text: Energy: 30 - 35 Kcal/Kg IBW/day

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Patients should be advised to consume 30 - 35 Kcal /Kg IBW/day in order to achieve and/or maintain IBW as well as appropriate nitrogen balance. Consideration should be given to energy gained through PD glucose absorption. Energy intakes can be reduced to 30 Kcal/kg/IBW/day in the elderly and patients with reduced activity (KDOQI 2006, RA 2010, BDA 2011).
texts.text: Patients should be advised to consume 30 - 35 Kcal /Kg IBW/day in order to achieve and/or maintain IBW as well as appropriate nitrogen balance. Consideration should be given to energy gained through PD glucose absorption. Energy intakes can be reduced to 30 Kcal/kg/IBW/day in the elderly and patients with reduced activity (KDOQI 2006, RA 2010, BDA 2011).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Protein: 1 - 1.2 g protein/Kg IBW/day
texts.text: Protein: 1 - 1.2 g protein/Kg IBW/day

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Patients should be advised to have a minimum protein intake of 1 - 1.2g protein/Kg IBW/day in order to meet increased requirements associated with PD (increase in basic requirements in addition to PD losses). At least 50% of protein intake should be HBV (High Biological Value). Requirements increase during peritonitis and patients should be encouraged to increase their protein intake to reflect this (BDA 2011); a patient information leaflet has been developed and is available on eLibrary (see Section 12, UHCW Association Records).
texts.text: Patients should be advised to have a minimum protein intake of 1 - 1.2g protein/Kg IBW/day in order to meet increased requirements associated with PD (increase in basic requirements in addition to PD losses). At least 50% of protein intake should be HBV (High Biological Value). Requirements increase during peritonitis and patients should be encouraged to increase their protein intake to reflect this (BDA 2011); a patient information leaflet has been developed and is available on eLibrary (see Section 12, UHCW Association Records).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Potassium: Maintain serum potassium between 3.5 - 5.5 mmol/L
texts.text: Potassium: Maintain serum potassium between 3.5 - 5.5 mmol/L

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All PD patients should maintain their serum potassium between 3.5 - 5.5 mmol/L. Any pre-existing dietary restrictions should be reviewed on commencement of dialysis. Reduced potassium diets may compromise nutrient intake and should only be used where appropriate. Non dietary causes of hyperkalaemia should be excluded prior to commencing dietary restriction e.g. inadequate dialysis, medications. Aim for a normal range of serum bicarbonate (22 - 29 mmol/L).
texts.text: All PD patients should maintain their serum potassium between 3.5 - 5.5 mmol/L. Any pre-existing dietary restrictions should be reviewed on commencement of dialysis. Reduced potassium diets may compromise nutrient intake and should only be used where appropriate. Non dietary causes of hyperkalaemia should be excluded prior to commencing dietary restriction e.g. inadequate dialysis, medications. Aim for a normal range of serum bicarbonate (22 - 29 mmol/L).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Phosphate: Maintain serum phosphate between 1.1 and 1.7 mmol/L
texts.text: Phosphate: Maintain serum phosphate between 1.1 and 1.7 mmol/L

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All PD patients should maintain their serum phosphate between 1.1 - 1.7 mmol/L (RA 2010). Dietary restriction and/or phosphate binders may be required. The phosphate binders used locally are: Calcium acetate (Renacet or Phosex), Calcium carbonate (Calcichew), Lanthanum carbonate (Fosrenol), Sevelamer carbonate (Renegel) or Sevelamer hydrochloride (Renvela). (See Appendix D). Calcium Acetate should be used as first line where appropriate (NICE 2013).
texts.text: All PD patients should maintain their serum phosphate between 1.1 - 1.7 mmol/L (RA 2010). Dietary restriction and/or phosphate binders may be required. The phosphate binders used locally are: Calcium acetate (Renacet or Phosex), Calcium carbonate (Calcichew), Lanthanum carbonate (Fosrenol), Sevelamer carbonate (Renegel) or Sevelamer hydrochloride (Renvela). (See Appendix D). Calcium Acetate should be used as first line where appropriate (NICE 2013).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Calcium: Maintain between 2.2 - 2.5 mmol/L (corrected for serum albumin)
texts.text: Calcium: Maintain between 2.2 - 2.5 mmol/L (corrected for serum albumin)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Calcium should be managed in line with PTH (see below). In the presence of hypercalcaemia all calcium therapies should be reviewed (agreed best practice).
texts.text: Calcium should be managed in line with PTH (see below). In the presence of hypercalcaemia all calcium therapies should be reviewed (agreed best practice).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Intact Parathyroid Hormone (iPTH): 8 - 38 pmol/L (2 - 9 times the upper laboratory target)
texts.text: Intact Parathyroid Hormone (iPTH): 8 - 38 pmol/L (2 - 9 times the upper laboratory target)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Treatment should be based on trends; patients with levels of iPTH above the target range should be assessed for hyperphosphatemia and hypocalcaemia (KDIGO 2009). Active vitamin D analogues calcitriol and alfacalcidol should be used to down regulate the PTH (KDIGO 2009, NICE 2009, RA 2010). Patients with an iPTH above the target range despite appropriate use of calcitriol or alfacalcidol should be prescribed paricalcitol. Patients with a PTH above 85pmol/L who are refractory to standard therapy (calcitriol, alfacalcidol and paricalcitol) should be considered for cinacalcet or if appropriate partial or total parathyroidectomy (NICE 2009).
texts.text: Treatment should be based on trends; patients with levels of iPTH above the target range should be assessed for hyperphosphatemia and hypocalcaemia (KDIGO 2009). Active vitamin D analogues calcitriol and alfacalcidol should be used to down regulate the PTH (KDIGO 2009, NICE 2009, RA 2010). Patients with an iPTH above the target range despite appropriate use of calcitriol or alfacalcidol should be prescribed paricalcitol. Patients with a PTH above 85pmol/L who are refractory to standard therapy (calcitriol, alfacalcidol and paricalcitol) should be considered for cinacalcet or if appropriate partial or total parathyroidectomy (NICE 2009).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Sodium: Aim <100 mmol sodium (<6g salt/d)
texts.text: Sodium: Aim <100 mmol sodium (<6g salt/d)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This should help to improve blood pressure control and minimise fluid gains. Salt substitutes that contain potassium salts should be avoided unless advised otherwise by your dietitian (Sign 2008).
texts.text: This should help to improve blood pressure control and minimise fluid gains. Salt substitutes that contain potassium salts should be avoided unless advised otherwise by your dietitian (Sign 2008).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Fluid: Aim 750ml + previous days urine output
texts.text: Fluid: Aim 750ml + previous days urine output

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Individual capacity for ultrafiltration must be considered. Sodium restriction will help to minimise fluid gains (RA 2010).
texts.text: Individual capacity for ultrafiltration must be considered. Sodium restriction will help to minimise fluid gains (RA 2010).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Vitamins & Minerals:
texts.text: Vitamins & Minerals:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: No specific criteria set. Consider water soluble vitamin supplementation, particularly vitamin C and folate for patients on restricted diets or those who have been identified as being nutritionally compromised. Avoid supplements containing fat soluble vitamins due to reduced excretion in dialysis patients and risk of toxicity. Suitable multivitamin preparations include Renavit and also standard pregnancy multivitamins.
texts.text: No specific criteria set. Consider water soluble vitamin supplementation, particularly vitamin C and folate for patients on restricted diets or those who have been identified as being nutritionally compromised. Avoid supplements containing fat soluble vitamins due to reduced excretion in dialysis patients and risk of toxicity. Suitable multivitamin preparations include Renavit and also standard pregnancy multivitamins.

texts.text: Definitions

texts.text: BMI = Body Mass Index

texts.text: CKD = Chronic Kidney Disease

texts.text: IBW = Ideal Body Weight iPTH = Intact Parathyroid Hormone

texts.text: PPI = Proton Pump Inhibitors

texts.text: PD = Peritoneal Dialysis

texts.text: PTH = Parathyroid Hormone

texts.text: PDQA = PD Quality Assurance

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline details
texts.text: Guideline details

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Assessment
texts.text: Assessment

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  All patients should be reviewed when they commence PD and followed up within two months. Thereafter, patients should be reviewed every six months. More frequent review may be necessary dependant on blood results or other identified dietetic needs.
texts.text:  All patients should be reviewed when they commence PD and followed up within two months. Thereafter, patients should be reviewed every six months. More frequent review may be necessary dependant on blood results or other identified dietetic needs.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  The dietitian will aim to attend multi-disciplinary PDQA clinics (3 per month), during which SGA and HGS measurements will be carried out where possible, and patients requiring further review will be identified.
texts.text:  The dietitian will aim to attend multi-disciplinary PDQA clinics (3 per month), during which SGA and HGS measurements will be carried out where possible, and patients requiring further review will be identified.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  All patients should be screened for malnutrition using the following nutrition screening guidelines;
texts.text:  All patients should be screened for malnutrition using the following nutrition screening guidelines;

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Actual Body Weight < 85% of Ideal Body Weight
texts.text:  Actual Body Weight < 85% of Ideal Body Weight

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Reduction in oedema free body weight (of 5% or more in 3 months or 10% or more in 6 months)
texts.text:  Reduction in oedema free body weight (of 5% or more in 3 months or 10% or more in 6 months)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  BMI (<20kg/m2)
texts.text:  BMI (<20kg/m2)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Subjective Global Assessment (SGA) (1 - 5 on 7-point scale)
texts.text:  Subjective Global Assessment (SGA) (1 - 5 on 7-point scale)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The above measures have been linked to increased mortality and other adverse outcomes (RA 2010).
texts.text: The above measures have been linked to increased mortality and other adverse outcomes (RA 2010).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Nutrient intakes
texts.text: Nutrient intakes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the following nutrient requirements Ideal Body Weight (IBW) should be calculated using a BMI of 20kg/m² if the patient's BMI is <20kg/m², a BMI of 25kg/m² if the patient's BMI is above 25kg/m² and actual BMI if the patient's BMI is between 20 and 25kg/m² (RA 2010).
texts.text: For the following nutrient requirements Ideal Body Weight (IBW) should be calculated using a BMI of 20kg/m² if the patient's BMI is <20kg/m², a BMI of 25kg/m² if the patient's BMI is above 25kg/m² and actual BMI if the patient's BMI is between 20 and 25kg/m² (RA 2010).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Energy
texts.text: Energy

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  30-35 kcal/kg IBW/day for all patients depending upon age and physical activity (RA 2010).
texts.text:  30-35 kcal/kg IBW/day for all patients depending upon age and physical activity (RA 2010).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Additional calories gained from peritoneal absorption of dialysate glucose should be included when estimating total energy intake (Appendix A).
texts.text:  Additional calories gained from peritoneal absorption of dialysate glucose should be included when estimating total energy intake (Appendix A).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Renal patients are at high risk of hyperlipidaemia and cardiovascular disease (CVD). As such, healthy eating guidelines should be followed where appropriate.
texts.text:  Renal patients are at high risk of hyperlipidaemia and cardiovascular disease (CVD). As such, healthy eating guidelines should be followed where appropriate.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Reduced intakes of 30 Kg/IBW/day may be appropriate in older adults (those >60 years of age), and patients with reduced activity (agreed best practice).
texts.text:  Reduced intakes of 30 Kg/IBW/day may be appropriate in older adults (those >60 years of age), and patients with reduced activity (agreed best practice).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Patients with CKD should ideally have a BMI of at least 20 and a maximum of 30 (agreed best practice). Individuals with a BMI > 30 or <20 should be referred to a dietitian for assessment.
texts.text:  Patients with CKD should ideally have a BMI of at least 20 and a maximum of 30 (agreed best practice). Individuals with a BMI > 30 or <20 should be referred to a dietitian for assessment.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Regular dietetic review will help to identify when modifications to energy intake are needed.
texts.text:  Regular dietetic review will help to identify when modifications to energy intake are needed.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Protein
texts.text: Protein

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Adequate dietary protein is required to meet basic requirements, to compensate for dialysis induced catabolism, and to replace peritoneal losses (4 - 12 g/d). Peritoneal losses can increase by up to 70% during peritonitis.
texts.text:  Adequate dietary protein is required to meet basic requirements, to compensate for dialysis induced catabolism, and to replace peritoneal losses (4 - 12 g/d). Peritoneal losses can increase by up to 70% during peritonitis.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Patients should be advised to have a minimum protein intake of 1 - 1.2 g protein/Kg IBW/day (BDA 2011).
texts.text:  Patients should be advised to have a minimum protein intake of 1 - 1.2 g protein/Kg IBW/day (BDA 2011).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  At least 50% of protein intake should be HBV (High Biological Value) (BDA 2011).
texts.text:  At least 50% of protein intake should be HBV (High Biological Value) (BDA 2011).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Requirements increase during peritonitis, aim for 1.5 g protein/Kg IBW/d.
texts.text:  Requirements increase during peritonitis, aim for 1.5 g protein/Kg IBW/d.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Any PD patients with peritonitis should be referred to the dietitian for dietary assessment. PD staff can provide the patient with the information sheet from eLibrary (PD peritonitis and diet) and supply them with some nutritional supplements (Fortisip Compact Protein) to trial, until the dietitian reviews them.
texts.text:  Any PD patients with peritonitis should be referred to the dietitian for dietary assessment. PD staff can provide the patient with the information sheet from eLibrary (PD peritonitis and diet) and supply them with some nutritional supplements (Fortisip Compact Protein) to trial, until the dietitian reviews them.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Potassium
texts.text: Potassium

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Maintain serum potassium levels at 3.5 - 5.5 mmol/L.
texts.text:  Maintain serum potassium levels at 3.5 - 5.5 mmol/L.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Patients should be reviewed by a dietitian on commencement of PD and given dietary advice.
texts.text:  Patients should be reviewed by a dietitian on commencement of PD and given dietary advice.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Reduced potassium diets may compromise nutrient intake and should only be used where appropriate, target 1 mmol/Kg IBW/day if needed.
texts.text:  Reduced potassium diets may compromise nutrient intake and should only be used where appropriate, target 1 mmol/Kg IBW/day if needed.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Non dietary causes of hyperkalaemia should also be considered (Appendix B).
texts.text:  Non dietary causes of hyperkalaemia should also be considered (Appendix B).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Aim for a normal range of serum bicarbonate (22 - 29 mmol/L).
texts.text:  Aim for a normal range of serum bicarbonate (22 - 29 mmol/L).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Where serum potassium levels are consistently <3.5 mmol/L  an increased potassium diet should be advised.
texts.text:  Where serum potassium levels are consistently <3.5 mmol/L  an increased potassium diet should be advised.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Phosphate
texts.text: Phosphate

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Maintain serum phosphate levels at 1.1 - 1.7 mmol/L.
texts.text:  Maintain serum phosphate levels at 1.1 - 1.7 mmol/L.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Dietary restriction and/or phosphate binders may be required.
texts.text:  Dietary restriction and/or phosphate binders may be required.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  The phosphate binders used locally are: Renacet, Phosex, Calcichew, Fosrenol, Renegel and Renvela.
texts.text:  The phosphate binders used locally are: Renacet, Phosex, Calcichew, Fosrenol, Renegel and Renvela.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Calcium Acetate should be the first line calcium-based phosphate binder (NICE 2013).
texts.text:  Calcium Acetate should be the first line calcium-based phosphate binder (NICE 2013).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Patients should be reviewed by a renal dietitian when commencing or changing phosphate binders to ensure appropriate advice is given regarding correct usage, timing and types of foods they need to be taken with.
texts.text:  Patients should be reviewed by a renal dietitian when commencing or changing phosphate binders to ensure appropriate advice is given regarding correct usage, timing and types of foods they need to be taken with.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Phosphate binders should not be taken at the same time as oral iron supplements as this will reduce the effectiveness of both medications. Calcium carbonate should be used with caution in patients on PPI's since this can affect efficacy.
texts.text:  Phosphate binders should not be taken at the same time as oral iron supplements as this will reduce the effectiveness of both medications. Calcium carbonate should be used with caution in patients on PPI's since this can affect efficacy.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Phosphate restriction can compromise nutritional adequacy, particularly protein intake and therefore should be given under dietetic supervision.
texts.text:  Phosphate restriction can compromise nutritional adequacy, particularly protein intake and therefore should be given under dietetic supervision.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  It may be difficult in some patients, especially vegetarians, to achieve an adequate protein intake on a low phosphate diet and this should be taken into consideration.
texts.text:  It may be difficult in some patients, especially vegetarians, to achieve an adequate protein intake on a low phosphate diet and this should be taken into consideration.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Calcium
texts.text: Calcium

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Maintain serum calcium levels at 2.2 - 2.5 mmol/L (corrected for serum albumin).
texts.text:  Maintain serum calcium levels at 2.2 - 2.5 mmol/L (corrected for serum albumin).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  If calcium levels are >2.5 mmol/L then all calcium based therapies should be reviewed.
texts.text:  If calcium levels are >2.5 mmol/L then all calcium based therapies should be reviewed.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  If a patient's serum calcium exceeds the upper limit of the target range then lower calcium dialysate should be considered.
texts.text:  If a patient's serum calcium exceeds the upper limit of the target range then lower calcium dialysate should be considered.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Calcium should be managed inline with iPTH (see below).
texts.text:  Calcium should be managed inline with iPTH (see below).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Intact Parathyroid Hormone
texts.text: Intact Parathyroid Hormone

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Treatment should be based on trends; patients with levels of iPTH above the target range should be assessed for hyperphosphatemia and hypocalcaemia (KDIGO 2009).
texts.text:  Treatment should be based on trends; patients with levels of iPTH above the target range should be assessed for hyperphosphatemia and hypocalcaemia (KDIGO 2009).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Abnormalities should be addressed by: reducing dietary phosphate intake and/or administering phosphate binders, calcium supplements and active vitamin D analogues; calcitriol or alfacalcidol (KDIGO 2009, NICE 2009, RA 2010).
texts.text:  Abnormalities should be addressed by: reducing dietary phosphate intake and/or administering phosphate binders, calcium supplements and active vitamin D analogues; calcitriol or alfacalcidol (KDIGO 2009, NICE 2009, RA 2010).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Patients with an iPTH above the target range despite appropriate use of calcitriol or alfacalcidol should be prescribed paricalcitol.
texts.text:  Patients with an iPTH above the target range despite appropriate use of calcitriol or alfacalcidol should be prescribed paricalcitol.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Patients with an iPTH above 85pmol/L who are refractory to standard therapy (calcitriol, alfacalcidol and paricalcitol) should be considered for cinacalcet or if appropriate partial or total parathyroidectomy (NICE 2009).
texts.text:  Patients with an iPTH above 85pmol/L who are refractory to standard therapy (calcitriol, alfacalcidol and paricalcitol) should be considered for cinacalcet or if appropriate partial or total parathyroidectomy (NICE 2009).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Sodium
texts.text: Sodium

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Dietary salt intakes >6g/d precipitate hypertension.
texts.text:  Dietary salt intakes >6g/d precipitate hypertension.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  All patients (with the exception of 'salt losers') should be advised on achieving a sodium intake of <100 mmol (6g salt) a day (SIGN 2008).
texts.text:  All patients (with the exception of 'salt losers') should be advised on achieving a sodium intake of <100 mmol (6g salt) a day (SIGN 2008).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Dietary sodium restriction is important for the regulation of sodium balance and will also aid adherence to a fluid restriction by reducing thirst.
texts.text:  Dietary sodium restriction is important for the regulation of sodium balance and will also aid adherence to a fluid restriction by reducing thirst.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Dietary sodium restriction should not be maintained at the expense of nutritional status. Temporary relaxation of the restriction may be appropriate to encourage improved oral intake (agreed best practice).
texts.text:  Dietary sodium restriction should not be maintained at the expense of nutritional status. Temporary relaxation of the restriction may be appropriate to encourage improved oral intake (agreed best practice).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Fluid
texts.text: Fluid

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Patients should be given advice to help them adhere to their fluid restriction (FR).
texts.text:  Patients should be given advice to help them adhere to their fluid restriction (FR).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Recommendations for FR are made by the medical team.
texts.text:  Recommendations for FR are made by the medical team.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  As a general guideline FR = 750ml + PDUO (Previous days urine output) (RA, 2010), however the ultrafiltration capacity of each patient will need to be taken into consideration.
texts.text:  As a general guideline FR = 750ml + PDUO (Previous days urine output) (RA, 2010), however the ultrafiltration capacity of each patient will need to be taken into consideration.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Sodium restriction will help to control fluid intake by reducing thirst.
texts.text:  Sodium restriction will help to control fluid intake by reducing thirst.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Fluid overload is often treated by increasing ultrafiltration, using hypertonic dialysis fluids (strong bags). Regular use of hypertonic fluids will increase the glucose load on the body and lead to a greater risk of obesity, hypertriglyceridaemia, as well potentially decreasing the lifespan of PD.
texts.text:  Fluid overload is often treated by increasing ultrafiltration, using hypertonic dialysis fluids (strong bags). Regular use of hypertonic fluids will increase the glucose load on the body and lead to a greater risk of obesity, hypertriglyceridaemia, as well potentially decreasing the lifespan of PD.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Vitamins and Minerals
texts.text: Vitamins and Minerals

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Little is known about the specific vitamin and mineral requirements of PD patients.
texts.text:  Little is known about the specific vitamin and mineral requirements of PD patients.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Dietary restrictions, early satiety, and periods of illness all impact on intake of some micronutrients.
texts.text:  Dietary restrictions, early satiety, and periods of illness all impact on intake of some micronutrients.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Water soluble vitamins, particularly folate and vitamin C are removed during dialysis.
texts.text:  Water soluble vitamins, particularly folate and vitamin C are removed during dialysis.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Micronutrient losses during dialysis may be offset to some extent by reduced urinary losses and altered renal metabolism.
texts.text:  Micronutrient losses during dialysis may be offset to some extent by reduced urinary losses and altered renal metabolism.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Consider water soluble vitamin supplementation, particularly vitamin C and folate for patients on restricted diets or those who have been identified as being nutritionally compromised.
texts.text:  Consider water soluble vitamin supplementation, particularly vitamin C and folate for patients on restricted diets or those who have been identified as being nutritionally compromised.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Avoid supplements containing fat soluble vitamins due to reduced excretion in dialysis patients and risk of toxicity. This includes fish liver oils. Suitable multivitamin preparations include Renavit and also standard pregnancy multivitamins.
texts.text:  Avoid supplements containing fat soluble vitamins due to reduced excretion in dialysis patients and risk of toxicity. This includes fish liver oils. Suitable multivitamin preparations include Renavit and also standard pregnancy multivitamins.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Dietitians should ensure patients are not following any unnecessary dietary restrictions. Where dietary intake is considered to be inadequate advice should be given on appropriate supplementation
texts.text:  Dietitians should ensure patients are not following any unnecessary dietary restrictions. Where dietary intake is considered to be inadequate advice should be given on appropriate supplementation

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Additional Considerations
texts.text: Additional Considerations

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Changes to dialysis regimen need to be considered with regards to calories gained from the dialysate.
texts.text:  Changes to dialysis regimen need to be considered with regards to calories gained from the dialysate.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Nutrineal solution is generally used by diabetic patients; this exchange needs to be undertaken around meal time. It can also be used when the protein intake is inadequate although patients should be well dialysed and monitored for signs of acidosis.
texts.text:  Nutrineal solution is generally used by diabetic patients; this exchange needs to be undertaken around meal time. It can also be used when the protein intake is inadequate although patients should be well dialysed and monitored for signs of acidosis.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Dialysis adequacy (ideally a combined urinary and peritoneal Kt/V urea of >1.7L/week or a creatinine clearance of >50L week/1.73m 2 minimal treatment (RA 2010). However, at UHCW we aim for a higher creatinine clearance of > 60L/wk for CAPD, >65L/wk for APD, and a Kt/V of 2.0L/week.
texts.text:  Dialysis adequacy (ideally a combined urinary and peritoneal Kt/V urea of >1.7L/week or a creatinine clearance of >50L week/1.73m 2 minimal treatment (RA 2010). However, at UHCW we aim for a higher creatinine clearance of > 60L/wk for CAPD, >65L/wk for APD, and a Kt/V of 2.0L/week.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Inadequate dialysis is likely to have a detrimental effect on nutritional status due to uraemia.
texts.text:  Inadequate dialysis is likely to have a detrimental effect on nutritional status due to uraemia.

texts.orig: End of clinical content
texts.text: End of clinical content

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Implementation
texts.text: Implementation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These guidelines have been disseminated and implemented through the dietetic and renal multidisciplinary meetings, and are on the e-library so that all members of the renal multidisciplinary team have access to them.
texts.text: These guidelines have been disseminated and implemented through the dietetic and renal multidisciplinary meetings, and are on the e-library so that all members of the renal multidisciplinary team have access to them.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Training
texts.text: Training

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All new renal dietitians have an introduction and training programme. Included within this is training on the implementation of the renal dietetic guidelines.
texts.text: All new renal dietitians have an introduction and training programme. Included within this is training on the implementation of the renal dietetic guidelines.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Renal Association Guidelines 2010; Screening for undernutrition in CKD
texts.text: Renal Association Guidelines 2010; Screening for undernutrition in CKD

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Screening methods for undernutrition in CKD
texts.text: Screening methods for undernutrition in CKD

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that all patients with stage 4-5 CKD should have the following parameters measured as a minimum in order to identify undernutrition (1C):
texts.text: We recommend that all patients with stage 4-5 CKD should have the following parameters measured as a minimum in order to identify undernutrition (1C):

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Actual Body Weight (ABW) (< 85% of Ideal Body Weight (IBW))
texts.text:  Actual Body Weight (ABW) (< 85% of Ideal Body Weight (IBW))

table_cells.text: Group / committee receiving report & responsible for actions
Excel Spreadsheet, colour coded to identify patients who are close to (or fall outside) of RA audit criteria (date of review, SGA and HGS).
Renal Clinical Forum / Renal Dietetics Team
Audit of dietetic records against proton timeline to check patients have been seen during PD training.

table_cells.text: Swallow whole (or can be snapped into two pieces) with meals
Lanthanum carbonate (Fosrenol) (500mg/750mg/1000mg chewable tablets and 750mg and 1000mg powder sachets)
To be taken at the end of meals or immediately after meals
Swallow whole or take powder mixed with 60mls water as a drink with meals
